title: Right and Wrong in AI

modules.title: What Makes an AI Decision Ethical
modules.sub: Exploring the values and principles behind AI choices
modules.description: Learners examine what it means for an AI system to make a 'good' or 'bad' decision by applying foundational ethical frameworks to familiar scenarios. They compare consequentialist, deontological, and virtue-based lenses to the same AI decision and weigh the trade-offs each perspective reveals. By the end, learners can articulate why ethics in AI is not one-size-fits-all.

modules.title: Fairness: Who Gets Left Out
modules.sub: Identifying who bears the cost of biased algorithms
modules.description: Learners investigate how AI systems can disadvantage specific groups through biased training data, flawed proxies, and unexamined assumptions. They audit simplified model outputs to spot patterns of exclusion and debate competing definitions of fairness that often mathematically conflict with one another. The module builds the habit of asking 'who is not represented here?' before accepting an algorithmic outcome.

modules.title: Transparency and the Black Box
modules.sub: Unpacking how explainability shapes trust in AI
modules.description: Learners explore why many powerful AI models resist easy explanation and what that opacity costs individuals and institutions. They work through real cases where lack of transparency led to harmful or unjust outcomes, then evaluate practical tools — such as LIME and SHAP — designed to surface reasoning. The module challenges learners to decide when 'it works' is an acceptable answer and when it is not.

modules.title: Accountability When AI Fails
modules.sub: Tracing responsibility across humans, systems, and organizations
modules.description: Learners map the chain of actors — developers, deployers, regulators, and end users — involved when an AI system causes harm, and grapple with the 'many hands' problem that makes blame diffuse. Through case reconstructions, they assign accountability at each link in the chain and propose governance structures that close responsibility gaps. The module ends with learners drafting a personal accountability checklist relevant to their own professional context.

modules.title: Consent and Data You Share
modules.sub: Examining what informed consent really means for data
modules.description: Learners dissect how personal data flows from collection through model training, often well beyond what users consciously agreed to. They evaluate real privacy policies and consent forms against a checklist of meaningful informed consent, identifying where language obscures rather than clarifies. The module empowers learners to make more deliberate data-sharing decisions and advocate for clearer consent practices in their organizations.

modules.title: AI Ethics in Real Scandals
modules.sub: Learning hard lessons from high-profile AI failures
modules.description: Learners analyze a curated set of documented AI controversies — including hiring algorithms, predictive policing tools, and facial recognition misidentification — as structured case studies. For each scandal, they identify the ethical failure mode, the stakeholders harmed, and the systemic conditions that allowed the problem to develop unchecked. The module transforms abstract principles into concrete pattern recognition that learners can apply when encountering new AI deployments.

modules.title: Building Your Ethical Framework
modules.sub: Synthesizing principles into a personal decision-making guide
modules.description: Learners consolidate insights from previous modules to construct their own structured ethical decision-making framework tailored to their role and industry. They stress-test the framework against three novel AI scenarios, refining it where it produces inconsistent or unsatisfying guidance. The result is a living document learners can return to and update as AI capabilities and social expectations evolve.

modules.title: Becoming an Ethical AI Citizen
modules.sub: Translating individual values into collective action and advocacy
modules.description: Learners shift from personal reflection to civic and organizational engagement, exploring how individuals can influence AI policy, workplace norms, and product decisions. They identify one concrete action — such as raising a concern, joining an advocacy group, or proposing an internal review process — and draft a brief commitment plan. The module closes by connecting personal ethical clarity to broader democratic participation in shaping how AI is governed.

synopsis: AI ethics isn't just for philosophers — every person affected by algorithms deserves to understand the values baked into them. This course introduces core ethical concepts through real-world cases and everyday decisions.
rationale: A demand score of 7/10 paired with only 0.61 similarity to the policy-focused 'AI and Democracy' confirms that a ground-up ethics course for general audiences remains a genuine and urgent gap in accessible AI literacy education.
